feed.title: Seems Fair
feed.subtitle: Questioning rules and decisions in human and AI systems

entry.title: The Cost of Sovereign AI
entry.content:
Last week I was at VivaTech, like at least half of Paris, and I was captivated by how much emphasis was placed on AI sovereignty. I followed several talks with growing interest, but panel after panel the narrative was the same: "the EU needs to catch up" in order to "win" or, depending on the speaker, at least "participate in the AI race".

Over the past few months, I've noticed this growing attention to sovereignty, with the term appearing more and more often on my LinkedIn feed. It reminds me of a similar pattern a few years ago, some of you might remember it, around the word resilience. And it's funny because, as I was making the comparison, I realized that most of the times sovereignty is used almost interchangeably with resilience.

Take, for example, what happened a couple of weeks ago, when the U.S. government restricted access to Anthropic's model Fable. Entire pipelines built around that model went dark overnight. The episode became a catalyst for a new wave of calls for sovereignty. But what people really meant in that context was reducing dependence on specific models. While I'm writing this article, Sakana AI has just released Fugu, an orchestrator of frontier models that claims to "match the performance of Fable and Mythos, delivering frontier capability without the risk of export controls". Unsurprisingly, it immediately attracted a great deal of attention, and the buzzword AI sovereignty accompanied almost every repost. But if sovereignty means control, this sounds like a step in the opposite direction. Not only are you not controlling the availability of the underlying models, you're not even controlling which model is being used for a given task. Here too sovereignty seems to be used as a synonym for resilience.

These two things matter but they're not the same. Resilience is about continuity: making sure your systems keep running when something breaks or gets cut off. Sovereignty is about control: deciding who gets to touch your infrastructure, your data, your models, and on what terms.

So what would AI sovereignty actually require? And what would it cost, in a geopolitical landscape this tense and a supply chain this globally fragmented?

Because I came out of VivaTech with more questions than answers, I decided to dig deeper and run a little intellectual experiment. In what follows, I'd like you to imagine that we're the president (and maybe also the entire cabinet) of Promptistan, a country that has decided, once and for all, to build its own sovereign AI.

Promptistan is a perfectly ordinary nation. It has engineers, a government with grand ambitions, a flag, and a budget. What it doesn't yet have is a large language model that is truly independent from any possible external disruption.

What would it take to achieve that goal?

With a proper engineer's mindset, I propose that we divide et impera the problem. A sovereign AI requires, at minimum, control over four things:

  1. the physical infrastructure to run it,
  2. the data to train it,
  3. the algorithms that define it,
  4. the energy to power it.
Part One

The Hardware

The first thing Promptistan will need is GPUs: the chips that do the actual mathematical heavy lifting of training and running an AI model. This seems manageable: GPUs are a product, we can buy them.

Except... one company makes almost all the GPUs that matter for AI. Nvidia holds roughly 80% to 90% of the AI accelerator market by revenue, with the remainder split between AMD, Intel, and a handful of custom silicon projects at Google, Meta, and Amazon.[1] If in Promptistan we want to train a frontier model tomorrow, we should, for all practical reasons, buy from Nvidia. But what if Nvidia doesn't have the capacity to supply the GPUs we need?

Moreover, there is something else we should consider. Technical data indicates that AI chips running at high utilization have a useful lifespan of roughly one to three years due to thermal stress and rapid technological obsolescence.[2] What if, in two years, geopolitical tensions, export controls, or market shifts mean we can no longer buy Nvidia hardware to replace failing machines or scale our infrastructure?

Fine, as Promptistan's Minister of Technology I'd say we build our own GPUs. How hard can it be?

Step one: Silicon

A chip begins as silicon. Specifically, as an ingot of ultra-pure silicon grown from melted sand, then sliced into extremely thin discs called wafers, polished to atomic smoothness, and used as the "canvas" on which a chip is built.[3]

Although sand is everywhere, including in Promptistan, semiconductor-grade high-purity quartz is extracted almost entirely from a single district: Spruce Pine, North Carolina, where two companies control roughly 95% of the world's supply.[4]

Once you have the silicon ingots, turning them into wafers is its own concentrated business. Two Japanese companies, Shin-Etsu and SUMCO, together control more than half the global silicon wafer market, with most remaining capacity held by Siltronic in Germany, GlobalWafers in Chinese Taipei, and SK Siltron in South Korea. Japan alone produces 55% of the world's silicon wafers.[5]

Step two: Rare earths

At this point in the conversation, someone always mentions rare earth elements. So let's be precise about what rare earths actually do in this supply chain.

Rare earth elements are a set of 17 nearly indistinguishable and hard to refine heavy metals. They are not really used in the chip itself, or at least not in large quantities. What they are mostly useful for are the machines that make the chip: the precision motors, the vacuum pumps, the positioning systems inside fabrication equipment all rely on permanent magnets made from rare earth compounds. These magnets are extraordinarily powerful for their size, and nothing currently available substitutes for them in high-precision applications.[6]

The name "rare earth" is also misleading. They're not particularly scarce in the ground, but they're rare in concentrated, economically extractable deposits, and they're notoriously difficult to separate from each other because the elements are chemically nearly identical. That makes refining them a long, expensive, and extremely toxic process.

China mines roughly 69% of the world's rare earths by volume, with the United States and Australia as the main alternatives. But the mining share understates China's actual leverage, because the real chokepoint is refining. In fact, China processes 91% of the world's rare earth supply. For the heavy rare earths like dysprosium and terbium, used in the most powerful magnets, the concentration is even more extreme, allowing China to produce 94% of the world's sintered permanent magnets.[7] For gallium and germanium, critical for compound semiconductor layers in radar, 5G, and some chip components, China produces approximately 98% of the world's refined supply.[8]

Even if Promptistan sits on abundant rare earth ore, it is in practice dependent on China to turn that ore into something usable.

On China's export controls

China has weaponised this refining dominance over the course of the last decade. In 2010, it banned rare earth exports to Japan during a fishing-trawler dispute.[9] In July 2023, it introduced licensing requirements for gallium and germanium. In December 2024, it banned outright sales of these materials to the United States. In October 2025, it expanded controls to cover rare-earth production equipment and process know-how itself, essentially attempting to export-control the very ability to build competing refining capacity.[10] Following US-China trade negotiations in November 2025, these controls were suspended for one year, with general licenses issued for US end-users, though explicitly framed as temporary.[11] In early 2026, China tightened restrictions specifically against Japan, following the Japanese Prime Minister's comments about Taiwan.[10] As I'm writing this article (22 June 2026), China's Ministry of Commerce added MP Materials and USA Rare Earth, the two companies most heavily backed by Washington to build an alternative supply chain, directly to its export control list.[12]

Step three: The machines that prints the chips

Suppose we've solved the materials problem and have silicon wafers and access to the refined elements we need. Now we need to actually print a chip. This requires photolithography: a process of using light to etch incredibly fine structures onto the silicon surface.

Modern AI chips require extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography, a technology that uses light with a wavelength of 13.5 nanometres, shorter than any natural light source on Earth, generated by firing a laser at tiny tin droplets 50,000 times per second.[13] It is, without exaggeration, one of the most complex machines humans have ever built. And exactly one company in the world makes it.

ASML, headquartered in Eindhoven in the Netherlands, holds 100% of the EUV lithography market and 94% of lithography overall.[14] ASML's EUV machines are currently operated only in Taiwan at TSMC, South Korea at Samsung, and in the United States and Europe at Intel. China's main chip manufacturer, SMIC, has never been permitted to buy one, first informally, then via explicit Dutch and US export controls.[15]

On the US export control of a Dutch company

How does the United States restrict sales made by a company headquartered in the Netherlands? Through a combination of diplomatic pressure and a technical chokehold. [16] ASML's EUV machines contain components manufactured in the US, which means they fall under US export control jurisdiction regardless of where ASML is based. Beyond that, the Dutch government, under significant pressure from Washington, has independently refused to renew ASML's export licences for EUV to China since 2019, and in 2023 extended those restrictions to some of its deep ultraviolet machines as well. The Netherlands has its own strategic reasons to comply: it is a NATO member, deeply integrated into US-led technology alliances, and ASML's biggest customers (TSMC, Samsung, Intel) are all in US-aligned countries. The result is that the export policy of a private Dutch company is effectively co-determined by Washington. China is finding ways to work around this. SMIC has been stockpiling older deep ultraviolet machines and has developed processes that squeeze near-frontier chip performance out of equipment that is technically a generation behind. Some analysts describe this as a slower, more expensive, but ultimately sanction-proof pathway.[17]

These machines cost around $200 million each for the current generation. ASML has just launched the next generation, called High-NA EUV, at roughly $400 million per machine. So expensive, in fact, that TSMC itself, the world's largest and most advanced chipmaker with a 2026 capital expenditure budget of $52 to $56 billion, publicly declined to buy them for its next two manufacturing nodes, with a senior vice president stating plainly that the price was too expensive.[18]

The best-funded, most technically capable chip manufacturer in the world looked at the most advanced chipmaking machine in the world and said: "well, we'll pass". Even setting aside cost, ASML will not sell to Promptistan without US and Dutch government approval.

Step four: Chip design and production

Before any of the above even begins, someone has to design the chip. This is done using Electronic Design Automation software, specialised tools that are themselves a chokepoint. US firms control roughly 96% of the EDA market, with Cadence and Synopsys as the main players. Core chip architectures, the fundamental instruction sets that chips understand, are concentrated in the US (x86, used in most computers) and the UK (Arm, used in almost every mobile device). Together, US and UK IP accounts for roughly 95% of the global market.[7]

Even if Promptistan had the design software, the materials, and somehow a lithography machine, it would still need a place to fabricate the chip at scale. The most advanced chips in the world are made almost exclusively by TSMC, which purchases roughly 80% of ASML's entire EUV output and manufactures chips for Nvidia, Apple, AMD, and most other leading chip designers. Samsung is a distant second for leading-edge nodes; Intel is rebuilding its own foundry capacity. Building a competitive foundry from scratch takes, at minimum, ten to fifteen years and tens of billions of dollars, and requires precisely the equipment, materials, and design tools we've just established Promptistan cannot easily access.

Part Two

The Data

Let's say Promptistan has, through some combination of diplomacy, luck, and very deep pockets, acquired the hardware it needs. Now it has to train its model. This requires data: vast amounts of text, in every format imaginable, scraped from the accumulated written output of human civilisation. Or better, Promptistani civilisation.

This is where things get philosophically interesting. Modern large language models are trained primarily on text from the internet. And the internet, it turns out, is not a neutral global commons. It is a deeply English-speaking, deeply American-shaped place. Over 90% of training data for most LLMs is in English, leaving every other language dramatically underrepresented.[19] To put a number on it: Llama 2, one of the most widely used open-source models, disclosed that 89.7% of its pre-training data was in English.[20] A filtered version of Common Crawl, developed by Google and known as the Colossal Clean Crawl Corpus, is one of the main raw data sources used to train these models. It has been estimated to consist of 51.3% of content hosted in the United States. Countries with the estimated 2nd, 3rd, 4th largest English speaking populations, India, Pakistan, Nigeria, and The Philippines have only 3.4%, 0.06%, 0.03%, 0.1% the URLs of the United States, despite having many tens of millions of English speakers.[21]

This matters for a reason that goes beyond linguistics. A model doesn't just learn language from its training data. It learns associations, assumptions, and values. It learns what a "normal" legal system looks like, what a "typical" family resembles, what counts as a reliable source of knowledge. When those implicit defaults are drawn from one culture's internet, the model encodes that culture's perspective as a baseline, and everything else as a deviation from it.[22]

So data sovereignty isn't really about where the servers are. It's about who decides what's true, whose history is the default, and whose perspective gets encoded as "objective".

In a true attempt at sovereignty, Promptistan trains on its own language and cultural data. This produces a model that speaks Promptistani fluently and reflects Promptistani culture. But it also reflects Promptistani history, Promptistani biases, and Promptistani blind spots, with no outside perspective to challenge them. But an AI trained only on what a society already believes is intelligence, or a very expensive mirror?

Part Three

The Algorithm

Here we can catch a breath. The algorithms are actually not the hardest part.

The architecture underlying every major language model today is the transformer, introduced in a 2017 paper titled "Attention Is All You Need", published by researchers at Google. It is freely available.[23] Anyone can read it, implement it, and build on it.

What isn't public is the engineering expertise required to implement transformers efficiently at enormous scale, the knowledge needed to squeeze performance out of expensive chips, and the fine-tuning techniques that turn a raw model into something actually useful. These are hard problems, but they're human capital problems. They can be built, taught, and developed, given time and investment.

Part Four

The Energy

There is a fourth pillar that gets less attention than materials and algorithms, but is just as foundational: energy. Training a frontier AI model means weeks of uninterrupted computation across thousands of chips, consuming electricity at a scale that would embarrass a small city.

Global data centers consumed 415 terawatt-hours of electricity in 2024, roughly 1.5 percent of total global demand, according to the International Energy Agency. In 2025, electricity demand from data centers alone soared by 17 percent, far outpacing overall global electricity growth of 3 percent. By 2030, the IEA projects that figure will roughly double to 945 terawatt-hours.[24] In the United States, data centers already consumed more than 4 percent of total national electricity in 2024, roughly equivalent to the annual demand of the entire country of Pakistan, and that figure could grow by 133 percent by 2030.[25]

Then there's water. Data centers cool themselves with enormous quantities of it. Training GPT-3 in Microsoft's US data centers directly evaporated 700,000 liters of freshwater on-site.[26] More recently, Google's data center in Council Bluffs, Iowa alone consumed 1.3 billion gallons of potable water in 2024.[27] Global AI-related water withdrawals could reach 4.2 to 6.6 billion cubic meters annually by 2027, equivalent to four to six times the yearly water consumption of Denmark.[28]

A country with an unstable electricity grid cannot train a competitive model: a power cut mid-training can ruin weeks of computation and hundreds of millions of dollars of GPU time. A country in a water-stressed region faces a different kind of constraint: the infrastructure of AI is thirsty in ways that compete directly with agriculture and drinking water.

Conclusion

What's the actual cost of sovereignty?

Turns out that Promptistan will have a hard time achieving true AI sovereignty. The further I pushed the thought experiment, and the research, the clearer it became that we're speaking about cutting ties in a fully entagled landscape. Every chip, every model, every query you send to an AI system is the product of North Carolina quartz, Chinese refining, Dutch optics, Taiwanese manufacturing, and American software, assembled, trained, and run using water and electricity that no single country owns. I'm not sure this entanglement is something to fight against, but simply the condition under which the technology exists.

At what point in this picture does "sovereign" become a meaningful description of anything?

Consider the environmental cost alone. Mining and refining the raw materials for a sovereign AI is not a clean, neutral act. For every tonne of rare earth produced, the mining process generates approximately 13 kilograms of dust, 9,600 to 12,000 cubic metres of waste gas, 75 cubic metres of wastewater, and one tonne of radioactive residue.[29] Rare earth ores are naturally laced with thorium and uranium, extracting the useful elements releases these into the surrounding environment.[30] At China's Bayan Obo mine in Inner Mongolia, the largest rare earth deposit on Earth, processing facilities have for decades discharged contaminated waste containing toxic chemicals, heavy metals, and radioactive thorium into tailings reservoirs, with pollutants seeping into surrounding soils and groundwater.[31] This cost is distributed on the whole humanity.

And then there is the epistemological cost. A model that reflects only the accumulated knowledge, assumptions, and blind spots of a particular society is not a window onto the world. It's a mirror of what already exists, and AI systems are not passive mirrors, they amplify and accelerate the patterns they learn. A model trained on a culturally homogeneous corpus does not just reflect that culture's biases, it reinforces them at scale.[32] The goal of sovereignty, pursued to its logical conclusion, produces an AI that is less useful, less accurate, and more ideologically closed than the global alternatives it was meant to replace.

None of this means that questions of AI governance, control, and dependency are unimportant. They are urgently important. The concentration of the chip supply chain and/or AI models in a handful of companies and countries is a geopolitical risk. The dominance of a few languages and cultures in AI training data is an equity problem. The environmental cost of mineral extraction and refinement and data center operation is an ethical problem.

But maybe a better question, a harder question, is not "how do we close ourselves off?" but: how do we participate in this global system with better rules, more equitable terms, and less damage to the places and people caught at the extractive end of the chain?

References
1Silicon Analysts, "NVIDIA AI GPU Market Share 2026" (April 2026) — siliconanalysts.com
2Center for Information Technology Policy, Princeton, "Lifespan of AI Chips: The $300 Billion Question" (October 2025) — blog.citp.princeton.edu
3Revolutionized, "Which Raw Materials Are Used in Semiconductor Chips?" (March 2026) — revolutionized.com
4smartecocity, "Semiconductor value-chain hidden champions 2026" (April 2026) — smartecocity.com
5OECD, "Mapping the semiconductor value chain" (June 2025) — oecd.org
6European Commission, "Rare Earth Elements, Permanent Magnets and Motors" (2026) — ec.europa.eu.
7IEA, "Rare Earth Elements" (2026) — iea.org
8USGS, "Minerals Yearbook 2024" — usgs.gov
9Reuters, "China bans rare earth exports to Japan after row-NYT" (September 2010) — reuters.com
10Andersen Institute, "China's Export Controls: Critical Minerals and Strategic Pressure Points" (April 2026) — anderseninstitute.org
11Pillsbury Law, "China Suspends Export Controls on Certain Critical Minerals" (November 2025) — pillsburylaw.com
12Reuters, "China targets U.S. rare earth and other firms with export controls" (June 2026) — reuters.com
13ASML, "EUV Lithography Systems" — asml.com
14TechMarketBriefs, "ASML Stock Analysis: EUV Monopoly, Earnings and Forecast" (April 2026) — techmarketbriefs.com
15Reuters, "Trump administration pressed Dutch hard to cancel China chip-equipment sale" (January 2020) — reuters.com
16MERICS, "Export Controls and the US-China Tech War" (March 2020) — merics.org
17American Enterprise Institute, "The Lithography Loophole: How China Is Printing Its Way to Chip Self-Sufficiency" (April 2026) — aei.org
18Bloomberg, "TSMC Says ASML's Latest Chipmaking Gear Is Too Pricey to Use" (April 2026) — bloomberg.com
19Tilde AI, "Building Multilingual LLMs: Why Data Quality Matters" (November 2025) — tilde.ai
20Shaham et al., "MEXA: Multilingual Evaluation of English-Centric LLMs via Cross-Lingual Alignment" (2024) — arxiv.org/abs/2410.05873
21 Dodge et al., "Documenting Large Webtext Corpora: A Case Study on the Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus" (2021) — https://arxiv.org/pdf/2104.08758
22Joshi et al., "Identity-Aware Large Language Models Require Cultural Reasoning" (2025) — arxiv.org/abs/2510.18510
23 Vaswani et al., "Attention Is All You Need" (2017) — arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
24International Energy Agency, "Energy and AI" (2026) and "Data centre electricity use surged in 2025" (April 2026) — iea.org
25Pew Research Center, "What we know about energy use at U.S. data centers amid the AI boom" (October 2025) — pewresearch.org
26Environmental Law Institute, "AI's Cooling Problem: How Data Centers Are Transforming Water Use" — eli.org
27Bashir et al., "Sustainable Open-Source AI Requires Tracking the Cumulative Footprint of Derivatives" (2025) — arxiv.org/abs/2601.21632
28Coroama et al., "Expert Assessment: The Systemic Environmental Risks of Artificial Intelligence" (2024) — arxiv.org/abs/2512.11863
29Harvard International Review, "Not So 'Green' Technology: The Complicated Legacy of Rare Earth Mining" (August 2021) — hir.harvard.edu
30Frontiers in Environmental Science, "Occurrence of uranium, thorium and rare earth elements in the environment: A review" (December 2022) — frontiersin.org
31Chatham House, "The rare earths race risks environmental disaster" (March 2026) — chathamhouse.org
32Zhu et al., "A Survey on Multilingual Large Language Models: Corpora, Alignment, and Bias" (2024) — arxiv.org/abs/2404.00929
entry.summary: Last week I was at VivaTech, like at least half of Paris, and I was captivated by how much emphasis was placed on AI sovereignty. I followed several talks with growing interest, but panel after panel the narrative was the same: "the EU needs to catch up" in order to "win" or, depending on the speaker, at least "participate in the AI race".

entry.title: “It’s not personal”… Is it ?
entry.content:
Picture this: an evening on the sofa, wrapped in a warm blanket, ready to watch the new episodes of The Traitors. The round table arrives, tensions rise, and then you hear it: “it’s not personal”.

If you opened this article, chances are you’re familiar with the show, which means you’ve heard this line more times than you can count (possibly second only to “omg, they played a blinder”). If not, you might want to pause here and binge all seasons before continuing. Don’t get me wrong, I think, for the most part, that’s true. Especially early in the game, there’s very little genuine animosity. People aren’t voting out of spite or revenge, they simply have to put a name down and they don’t yet have much to go on. No one’s slipped up, no big mistakes, and yet, somehow, certain people are already “giving traitor energy”.

Still, “not personal” doesn’t necessarily mean neutral. When decisions are made on instinct, what feels like a harmless gut feeling can quietly follow familiar social patterns.

A lot of viewers picked up on this feeling, and eventually The Times did too. They recently published a thoughtful piece exploring whether players on The Traitors might be influenced by prejudice, particularly around race, gender, and age. I appreciated that they took these concerns seriously and that they looked at the data. Also, the article prompted further discussion elsewhere, which helped raise awareness and open up a broader debate.

Reading it pushed me to dig a little deeper myself. I wasn’t entirely convinced by some of the methodological choices made, and I kept wondering what the data might say if approached slightly differently. But before getting into the results, I want to explain the intuition behind my choices and lay out a few important premises.

Premises

We need to keep in mind that The Traitors is, first and foremost, a reality TV show. As viewers at home, we see only a small, and heavily edited, slice of what actually happens. Context is missing, conversations are compressed, and some moments may even be “shaped” for narrative effect. This analysis is based solely on what is visible on screen. It is not, in any way, intended as a judgment of individual players or their personal behavior.

Public Tool: To keep the process transparent and fun, I’ve made the data-analysis tool publicly available, so you can explore and play with the data yourself here.

Gender and racial data were inferred using AI-based name analysis and may not reflect how contestants self-identify. Where possible, I cross-referenced these inferences with publicly available self-identification data, but inaccuracies are still possible. For the purposes of this analysis, the term People of Color is used to mean non-white, in line with the APA Style guidelines on bias-free language. This was a personally challenging exercise, and I genuinely welcome corrections or suggestions to improve accuracy. Full datasets and code are also available on GitHub.

Banishment vs Murder

The first thing that didn’t quite sit right with me was treating murders and banishments as the same kind of event. Murders are strategic decisions made by the Traitors behind closed doors. Banishments, especially early ones, are public, rushed, and based on very little information. Those first round tables are full of people openly admitting they’re “going with their gut”. If unconscious bias is going to show up anywhere, that feels like the most likely place. So I wanted to see if by focusing solely on banishments we would get different results.

Civilians vs Celebrities

The second choice I wanted to revisit was the inclusion of the celebrity season. Celebrities don’t enter the game as blank slates. They come with public personas, reputations, and sometimes existing relationships. That almost certainly introduces different kinds of bias but not necessarily the ones being examined here. This is also why, for now, I’m focusing only on the UK version of the show, as it’s the only one with enough diversity and a cast made entirely of civilians.

Votes vs Banishment

Finally, as a huge fan of the show and someone with a PhD in preference aggregation, I couldn’t help but thinking at the votes casted rather than the outcome of the round table itself. Let me explain. In the most recent season, for example, Amanda intensely focused on Jade from the very beginning, with very little justification. Jade made it to the final, but she was under suspicion the entire time. To me, that persistent targeting matters, even if it doesn’t lead to banishment. Because banishment works on plurality, a small but concentrated block of votes can change outcomes without necessarily revealing the full picture of bias.

With all that in mind, let’s look at the data!

Early Banishments Analysis

Banishment vs Murder

To test my intuition that banishments are fundamentally different from murders, I looked at banishments in the first four episodes (the same cutoff used by The Times) across all seasons, including the celebrity one.

Race: Players of Color make up 28.8% of the starting cast, yet account for 46.7% of early banishments.

Gender: The starting cast is almost perfectly balanced by gender. Still, women make up 53.3% of early banishments. It’s not a massive difference on its own, but combined with the race data, it suggests that early perceptions of who seems “trustworthy” or “expendable” may be shaped by demographic factors rather than strategy alone.

Interestingly, even when looking only at banishments, the overall patterns are very similar to those reported by The Times. Players of Color are still disproportionately represented among early exits, and women are still more likely to be eliminated early. In other words, while I still think banishments deserve separate attention, grouping murders and banishments together doesn’t dramatically change the headline result.

Who Survives the Longest?

Civilian vs Celebrity Seasons

My second concern was the inclusion of the celebrity season. Because celebrity players arrive with established public images, and often preexisting relationship, these factors likely dampen some forms of unconscious bias examined here. The Times also acknowledged this, excluding the celebrity season from their finalist race analysis.

Across civilian seasons:

  • White contestants consistently survive longer than Players of Color. White men have the longest average survival (nearly 8 episodes). Women of color have the shortest (just over 6 episodes).
  • Men are more likely, on average, to reach the finale (56.7% vs. 43.3%).
  • White contestants are disproportionately represented among finalists (81.7% compared to 18.3% Players of Color).

In the celebrity season:

  • Men still survive longer on average, but the gap is much narrower. Men of color have the longest average survival (around 7 episodes), while white women have the shortest (just over 5).
  • The racial pattern flips entirely in the finale: 60% Players of Color versus 40% white contestants.

This supports the hypothesis that the celebrity season does not follow the same bias patterns as civilian seasons.

Age Analysis

Age offers another lens on how first impressions and group instincts play out. Older contestants are a smaller portion of the starting cast, less than 10% are over 60, and around 11% are aged 45–59, while contestants under 30 make up the largest group at about 43%.

When we look at average survival contestants under 30 last just over 8 episodes on average. Those aged 30–44 survive around 7.7 episodes. Survival drops for older players: 45–59 year olds average 6 episodes, and those over 60 just over 5.

However, because of smaller sample sizes and potential game-mechanics confounds (e.g. performance in missions), I personally suggest to read this as suggestive rather than definitive evidence of age-based bias.

Votes vs. Banishments

My final question was whether voting tells a different story from banishments.

At the very first major round table, the gender split in the room is perfectly even: 50% men and 50% women. Despite this, voting behavior diverges sharply. A striking 76.8% of all votes are cast against women, with men receiving just 23.2%. That gap is far too large to be explained by representation alone and suggests that, in the earliest phase of the game, women are significantly more likely to be perceived as suspicious or expendable. This pattern holds across all seasons except the third, where the gap is smaller.

The racial composition at this stage is 73.2% white and 26.8% Players of Color, and voting by race mirrors this baseline: white players receive 69.5% of votes, and Players of Color 30.5%. The racial gap exists, but it’s smaller than the gap in banishments. Because the banishments are decided by plurality, even a relatively small number of concentrated votes can still have a big impact.

Interestingly, looking at round tables two and three (extending the analysis to episode four), the gender gap in voting narrows, while racial disparities in targeting tend to widen. This suggests a shift in how suspicion is socially distributed as the game progresses.

Conclusions

So, is it “not personal”? In the narrow sense the players mean it, probably yes. Most early decisions on aren’t driven by malice, vendettas, or conscious prejudice. They’re made under pressure, with little information, and a strong sense that someone has to go.

But the data suggests that “not personal” doesn’t mean “random”.

Across civilian seasons, early banishments and voting patterns consistently fall harder on certain groups (mainly women and Players of Color) well before there is meaningful strategic information to justify those choices. When people rely on gut feelings, those instincts appear to draw on familiar social shortcuts about who seems suspicious, trustworthy, or expendable.

Examining votes rather than just outcomes reinforces this: even when someone isn’t banished, persistent targeting reveals who is being framed as suspicious from the very start.

The contrast with the celebrity season strengthens this interpretation. When players enter the game with established public identities and reputations, many of these patterns weaken or even reverse. That suggests the biases observed in civilian seasons aren’t inevitable features of the game itself, but products of first impressions.

None of this means individual players are doing something wrong, nor does it imply deliberate bias. What it does show is how quickly social perceptions can crystallize when information is scarce and how those perceptions can quietly shape collective decisions.

In other words, The Traitors may not be personal but it is human, and when humans vote on instinct, the data suggests those instincts are rarely neutral.
entry.summary: Picture this: an evening on the sofa, wrapped in a warm blanket, ready to watch the new episodes of The Traitors. The round table arrives, tensions rise, and then you hear it: “it’s not personal”.
